1.explanation: Forces F1 and F2 point in the same direction, so they are a special case of vector addition: you can simply add their magnitudes (count the squares). Two parallel forces in the same direction yield a vector of the same direction but with a length equal to their sum.

diagrams.title: Vector addition: parallel and opposite special cases

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: when forces act in the same direction, you can just add them up (F1+F2 = 6 squares = R3).

2.explanation: Ripstop is a weaving pattern where thicker, stronger threads are inserted at regular intervals in both perpendicular directions of the weave. If a tear starts in the lighter weave, those reinforcement threads form a stop-grid that prevents the tear from running further than the next intersection. It's a structural weave property, not a coating or reinforcement patch.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: ripstop is woven in warp and weft - barriers in the cloth that stop a tear from propagating.

3.explanation: Ripstop is a weaving technique that inserts thicker, stronger threads at regular intervals in both warp and weft, creating tiny barriers across the cloth. A tear that starts can only propagate as far as the next barrier before being stopped. Coatings, fine weaves and elasticity don't stop tear propagation the way ripstop barriers do.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: ripstop is a weaving technique in warp and weft - small barriers in the cloth that stop a tear from spreading.

4.explanation: Porosity describes how readily air passes through the cloth. A new, well-coated wing has very low porosity, so it holds pressure cleanly and keeps its profile. As the coating ages porosity rises, performance drops and the wing tends to stall earlier. Porosity has nothing to do with tear resistance or bending.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: porosity is how much air the cloth lets through - if it lets too much through, the wing loses performance and speed.

5.explanation: Low porosity is essential: the canopy must hold air pressure to keep its shape and develop lift. A porous wing leaks and the pressure differential between upper and lower surface collapses - so the wing flies slower, stalls earlier and is more collapse-prone. Coatings exist specifically to drive porosity to the lowest practical value.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: if the wing lets too much air through, it loses performance and speed.

6.explanation: The coating's job is twofold: seal the weave so air can't leak through (low porosity, profile stays sharp) and fix the threads so the fabric doesn't stretch under load (low elasticity, wing keeps its certified shape). It also adds UV protection. Coatings don't increase mechanical strength as a primary effect - they preserve the cloth's properties.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the coating ensures UV resistance, low air permeability and even surface uniformity.

7.explanation: Paraglider cloth is coated (typically with polyurethane, sometimes silicone or Mylar) to seal the weave and stop air leaking through. Lower porosity means the canopy keeps a clean pressure differential between upper and lower surface, so the wing keeps its profile and performance. The coating also dampens UV ageing and can even improve mechanical strength a touch.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the coating seals the cloth so it doesn't react too strongly to UV and especially doesn't let too much air through.

8.explanation: Coatings (polyurethane, silicone or Mylar) shield the bare nylon threads from UV radiation. Without the coating, sun ages the fabric quickly - fibres become brittle, porosity climbs and the wing's flight characteristics drift. The coating also reduces porosity and elasticity; all three effects work together to keep a new wing flying like new for longer.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the coating ensures the wing doesn't react too strongly to UV and especially doesn't let too much air through.

9.explanation: Coating the woven nylon fixes the threads in place and stiffens the fabric, which directly reduces its elasticity. Low elasticity is wanted: the wing must keep a stable profile in flight rather than ballooning or stretching under aerodynamic load. The coating also lowers porosity and shields the cloth from UV.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the coating stabilises the cloth and stops it letting too much air through.

10.explanation: The wing's coating primarily lowers porosity, reduces elasticity and protects against UV. Adding any film to the woven cloth fixes the threads in place rather than letting them work independently, which in this exam framing is sometimes described as a small reduction in raw cloth mechanical strength. Either way, the practical effect: a sealed, profile-stable wing that ages less quickly.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the coating seals the cloth so it doesn't react too strongly to UV and especially doesn't let too much air through.

11.explanation: When the coating ages or is damaged, porosity rises and the wing loses internal pressure. The boundary layer separates earlier, so the wing stalls at a higher airspeed than its certified figure, and parachutal stalls become more frequent (the wing falls vertically without forward speed). That's why coating health is so closely tied to safe flight envelope.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: porosity matters - if the wing lets too much air through, it loses performance and speed.

12.explanation: Friction against rough surfaces (tar, gravel, concrete, sand) abrades the thin coating off the ripstop weave. Once the coating is worn through, porosity climbs and the wing's flight behaviour degrades. Cool, dark, dry storage protects the coating; extreme manoeuvres don't damage it directly.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: mechanical load and friction on the ground are bad for all material - never drag the wing.

13.explanation: Household detergents and cleaning sprays contain solvents and surfactants that chemically attack the polyurethane or silicone coating. Even a single wipe-down with the wrong product strips the seal and raises porosity. Always clean a wing only with lukewarm water - no soap, no spray, no detergent.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: clean with lukewarm water, but not with cleaning agents - those attack the coating.

14.explanation: Of the four drag factors (surface area, airflow speed, air density, shape), the speed of the airflow is the only one listed among the options. Relative humidity, material properties and weight have no effect on drag. Crucially, speed enters the drag equation as v squared, so it dominates: doubling speed quadruples drag.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: drag formula has shape, density, velocity squared and area - nothing else.

15.explanation: Speed enters the drag equation squared, so doubling the airflow speed multiplies the drag by 2 squared = 4. This is one of the most-tested concepts on the SHV exam - the big red exclamation mark, as the instructor puts it. Linear scaling (area, density) versus quadratic scaling (speed) is what catches most candidates out.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: big red flag - velocity is squared. Double speed = 4x drag. Triple speed = 9x drag.

16.explanation: Drag scales with the square of speed: tripling the airflow speed multiplies drag by 3 squared = 9. So a 30 km/h wind generating some drag F becomes 9F at 90 km/h. The exam trap is offering '12' (which would be 3 times 4) or '6' (which would be 3 times 2); only the squared scaling gives the right answer.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: 30 km/h to 90 km/h tripled the wind, so drag goes from 100 N not to 300 N but to 900 N.

17.explanation: Salt and the acids that come with seawater eat the coating, so the first priority is to flush them out. Rinse the wing thoroughly with fresh water as soon as possible, then dry it in the shade. Reverse-launching to dry or just shaking out the salt won't remove the corrosive residue from the fabric.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: cow dung and similar - clean with lukewarm water, never with cleaning agents, because those attack the coating.

18.explanation: Lift is generated by the pressure drop on the upper surface, and that suction is strongest just behind the leading edge - that's where the airflow is accelerating most over the curved profile. So the upper surface near the front carries the biggest aerodynamic load, which is why cell walls and line attachments are heaviest reinforced in that region.

19.explanation: Wind speed doubles from 30 to 60 km/h, and drag scales as v squared, so drag is multiplied by 4: 300 N becomes 1200 N. The area and altitude information is unchanged in this question - only speed changes, and speed dominates because it is squared.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: when only speed changes - and it doubles - drag quadruples (v squared).

20.explanation: The cell walls and ribs are the wing's internal structure. They take the concentrated load coming up each suspension line and spread it across the curved upper surface (which generates lift), so the cloth doesn't have to carry the load only at the line attachment points. Without them the upper surface would collapse into the line points like a sagging tent.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: cells, partition walls and crossports give the wing its shape and internal pressure.

21.explanation: Lift is generated mostly on the front portion of the wing's upper surface, so the line tension that must transfer that lift to the pilot peaks there. The cell wall takes its highest concentrated load at the line attachment points on the front half - which is also why those areas are specifically reinforced in modern wings.

22.explanation: In a B-stall the wing is pulled into a stalled descent while the A and C/D lines still hang against full weight - so the cell walls between line groups carry huge concentrated loads as the wing fights to stay open against the artificially imposed shape. Spiral dives and big ears load the lines more than the walls; thermals are gentle by comparison.

23.explanation: Where lines attach, the cell wall fabric carries concentrated point loads that try to pull and stretch the cloth. Manufacturers reinforce just those small areas - often with Mylar tape or extra-strong patches - so the cell wall doesn't deform around the attachment. Cross-ports and profile thickness don't address this localised stretch.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: cells, partition walls and crossports - the internal structure that gives the wing its shape.

24.explanation: Diagonal V-ribs internally connect upper to lower surface and redistribute load sideways - so a single line attachment can carry the load of several cells via the V-rib bridge. That means fewer suspension lines coming out the bottom of the wing, which directly reduces aerodynamic drag. It's a modern construction trick that boosts performance without sacrificing structure.

25.explanation: Air density is linear in the drag equation: halving density halves the drag. This is why drag drops as you fly higher - at altitude there are fewer air molecules to resist motion. Note the contrast with speed, which is squared, not linear.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: halving the density halves the drag - it's linear like surface area.

26.explanation: More cells at the same surface area means more internal walls - so the wing approximates the ideal airfoil more accurately (smoother profile), but the additional cell walls add weight and fold to a larger pack volume. Aspect ratio is set by span over area, which is identical here. The classic trade: precision and looks vs. weight and pack size.

27.explanation: Ripstop fabric is strong along the warp and weft thread directions - that's where the woven threads carry tension. But diagonal (bias) loads stretch the weave by shearing the squares, which over time deforms the fabric and changes the profile. Manufacturers package the cloth panels so most flight loads align with the threads, not the diagonal.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: diagonal load is what's not so liked - there it tends to stretch, so the panels are packaged to load along the threads.

28.explanation: Every stitch is a needle perforation in the fabric. If stitches are placed too close together, the line of holes acts like a perforated tear-strip and the material rips along it under load. Manufacturers space stitches carefully so the cloth keeps its strength between holes. Closer stitching doesn't reduce porosity or improve tear resistance.

29.explanation: Line groups are officially named by where they attach to the wing: A at the leading edge, then B, C, D toward the trailing edge, plus the brake (F) at the trailing edge. Manufacturers often colour-code groups for convenience (school wings frequently use red on the A group), but colour and diameter and material can all vary - the attachment point is the universal definition.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the sheath lets you colour-separate lines - especially school wings use red on the A group.

30.explanation: The A lines run from the front (A) riser up to the leading edge of the wing - the part of the canopy with the cell openings that the air enters through. Then come B, C and D moving back, with the brake (F) line attaching at the trailing edge. A-line attachment at the leading edge is what lets you pull up the wing during launch.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the brake line is at the trailing edge, then C, B, A riser groups - A is at the front, where the air enters.

31.explanation: Modern lines must be (1) as inelastic as possible so the wing keeps its profile under load, (2) strong enough to carry the loads with safety margin, and (3) as thin as possible to minimise parasitic drag. Those three are essentially incompatible without high-performance fibres, which is why Dyneema and aramid replaced everything else.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: Dyneema has very good weight-to-strength ratio - light, holds a lot, easy to process.

32.explanation: The accepted tolerance for modern paraglider lines is about 3 cm extension on a 6 m line under a load of 50 N (about 5 kg) - that's roughly 0.5% stretch. Modern lines are not zero-stretch (no real material is), but they are deliberately engineered to be very close to inelastic so the wing's profile and trim stay consistent.

33.explanation: Modern paraglider line cores are made of either polyethylene (brand name Dyneema, white, excellent strength-to-weight, slight stretch) or aramid (Technora/Kevlar, light brown, very dimensionally stable but kink-sensitive and UV-sensitive). Polyester is only used for the sheath; polyamide (nylon) is the wing fabric. Manufacturers typically use both core types in a mix.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the white line is Dyneema (polyethylene), the brown is aramid (Technora, Kevlar) - that's the line material mix.

34.explanation: The sheath (braided nylon or polyester) wraps the load-bearing core to shield it from UV radiation and from mechanical abrasion against other lines or terrain. A bare core would degrade in sunlight and fray on contact. The sheath does add diameter and drag, which is why some high-performance wings use unsheathed lines coated for UV protection instead.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the sheath protects the core from mechanical load and lets you colour-code the lines, but it thickens the line.

35.explanation: Even when the load-bearing core is dimensionally very stable, the polyester sheath wrapped around it shrinks over time - especially when exposed to moisture. The shrinking sheath pulls the assembled line shorter, particularly in lightly-loaded positions (C and D) where each line only carries 300-500 g and can't resist the sheath contraction.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: lines can shrink especially when not under load, the sheath drives that.

36.explanation: Sewing a loop into an aramid (Kevlar/Technora) line damages the rigid, kink-sensitive fibres at each needle hole - and can cut the line's strength by up to 40%. That's why splicing is used instead for aramid: the cores are intertwined without piercing the fibres, so almost no strength is lost.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: aramid (Technora, Kevlar) is dimensionally stable but kink-sensitive - you have to be careful where you install it.

37.explanation: For unsheathed aramid lines, splicing (intertwining the fibres of the core into a loop) doesn't pierce or damage the fibres, so it preserves nearly the full breaking strength. Sewing pierces the rigid fibres and can cost up to 40% of the line's strength. Fusing melts aramid (which has a high melting point) and isn't a safe loop technique.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: aramid is dimensionally stable but kink-sensitive - splicing rather than sewing.

38.explanation: When water gets into Dyneema or Aramid lines and then freezes, the ice expands inside the fibre bundle and damages the core - producing 'stress whitening' that is invisible from outside (hidden under the sheath) but seriously weakens the line. Normal manoeuvres or maximum load flying don't cause this. It's a care issue, not a flying issue: never store a wet wing where it might freeze.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the sheath protects the core - but damage inside the core stays hidden.

39.explanation: A thinner line of the same strength is essentially the same fibre core with less sheath bulk - so it has less aerodynamic drag (smaller frontal area) but is more flexible and slippery, which makes it more prone to tangling during wing preparation. That's why high-performance wings often use unsheathed thin lines but ask for careful sorting before launch.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: unsheathed lines have low weight and little drag - that's why they're used.

40.explanation: About 80% of the wing's load travels through A and B risers and only 20% through C and D - so A+B carry roughly four times the load of C+D, easily more than double. This isn't an equal distribution by design: it follows directly from the fact that lift is concentrated on the front of the wing.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: 80% of the load is on the A and B group, only 20% on C and D - that's why C/D lines barely see any tension.

41.explanation: Looped attachments are simply hooked into a sewn loop on the cell wall, so a damaged line can be unhooked and replaced quickly. Sewn-to-cloth attachments require seamstress work to repair. Looped connections aren't more aerodynamic or stronger - the benefit is purely serviceability, which is a big deal for a part that gets replaced periodically.

42.explanation: Lines are matched to a specific wing model: the manufacturer chose the diameter, sheathing, splice technique and material mix to give the wing its certified flight characteristics. Substituting different lines - even if they're stronger or the same diameter - changes how loads distribute and voids the certification. Only original parts via authorised service.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the line mix is what makes the wing fly like it was newly built - you don't change it casually.

43.explanation: Different lines see different loads in normal flying - A and B carry 80%, C and D only about 20%. Lines under more load tend to creep longer, lightly-loaded lines tend to shrink. So after many flights, the original lengths drift purely from normal use. That's why periodic glider checks include line-length measurement and trim correction.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

video_clips.snippet: Sam: lines stretch or shrink in normal use - you measure and trim 1-2 cm to bring the wing back like new.

44.explanation: A short, severe overload (line caught at launch, snagged on landing) stretches the fibres past their elastic limit. The line gets longer permanently, which shifts the geometry of that line group and subtly changes the wing's profile and flight behaviour. The line may still hold its rated load - the issue is dimensional, not strength.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

video_clips.snippet: Sam: lines deform - get longer or shorter - which you measure during a check and correct by trimming.

45.explanation: Dyneema and aramid line cores - and the polyester sheaths - shrink over time when exposed to moisture and dirt, especially in lightly-loaded positions (C and D, where each line only carries 300-500 g). The contamination penetrates the sheath and the unloaded fibres pull tighter as they dry. Cold or disuse don't cause permanent shortening.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: Dyneema lines in the C and D area, lightly loaded, tend to shrink - moisture amplifies that.

46.explanation: The trailing edge is the rear-most, sharp edge of the wing profile - where the airflows from above and below rejoin and where the brake line pulls down to change the angle of attack. The starting vortex forms at the trailing edge and contributes to lift through the circulation theory.

workbook_chapter.title: Lift and the profile of a wing

diagrams.title: Geometric points of a wing profile

47.explanation: Pulling the leading edge down by shortening A lines tilts the wing nose-low and reduces the angle of attack. Both the wing profile and its handling change - the wing flies faster, is easier to inflate, but is more prone to frontal collapses. A and D are opposite levers on the same AoA dial.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

48.explanation: The B lines sit in the middle of the line tree, so shortening them mostly bows the profile rather than shifting the leading edge up or down - the angle of attack barely changes. What does change is the wing's profile shape and how it handles. Not all line-group changes shift AoA; the B group is the exception.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

49.explanation: Stretching the rear (D) lines lowers the trailing edge, which tilts the wing nose-down and reduces the angle of attack. The wing's profile shape and handling change accordingly: faster flight, easier inflation, but more vulnerable to frontal collapses. Lengthening D mirrors what shortening A would do.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

50.explanation: Shortening the rear (D) lines tilts the back of the wing down, raising the leading edge - so the angle of attack increases. That changes both the wing's profile and how it handles: slower flight, harder to inflate, with parachutal-stall tendency. Any change in line-group length changes the profile.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

51.explanation: The angle of attack is measured between the chord line and the relative airflow. The profile in the figure showing the chord most steeply pitched up relative to the airflow has the largest positive angle of attack. Look for the profile where the leading edge is highest above the airflow direction.

workbook_chapter.title: Angle of attack and the polar of forces

52.explanation: Stretching the rear (D) lines drops the trailing edge, which tilts the wing nose-down and reduces the angle of attack. The wing inflates more easily and flies faster, but with a lower AoA the frontal collapse risk goes up while parachutal-stall risk drops. D and A behave opposite to each other.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the line can lengthen or shorten, you measure it in the check and trim 1-2 cm to bring it back like new.

53.explanation: Shortening the A lines pulls the leading edge down, reducing the angle of attack. The wing inflates more easily and flies faster, but with a lower AoA it's much more vulnerable to frontal collapses - and the parachutal-stall risk drops because the wing is further from the stall side of the polar. A and D have opposite effects.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

54.explanation: Shortening the rear (D) lines tilts the back of the wing down and raises the angle of attack. The wing now resists inflating, flies slower (because the higher AoA puts it closer to the stall side of the polar), is less prone to frontal collapses but much more likely to enter a parachutal stall. D and A produce opposite effects.

diagrams.title: Line length affects angle of attack

55.explanation: Lines are critical structural elements - a torn line means the wing's geometry and load distribution are compromised. Never tie lines back together, splice with random cord, or just fly without one: only an authorised replacement by an original line restores the safety margin. Ground the wing until it's properly repaired.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: lines hold a lot - you don't just replace them with anything.

56.explanation: Longer suspension lines put the pilot further below the wing, lowering the centre of gravity of the pilot-wing system. The longer 'pendulum arm' also means larger swinging amplitudes when the wing changes direction quickly. Short lines give crisper handling; long lines give a more pendulous, slower-feeling glider.

57.explanation: Reducing the angle of attack means accelerating: the wing pitches forward, more like a dart, and flies faster. From best glide, a 2-degree reduction in AoA brings the wing into accelerated trim - the trade-off is more speed but a worse glide ratio.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: accelerate the wing and the angle of attack decreases, Cl and Cd values decrease.

58.explanation: On the polar curve of forces, the lift coefficient Cl rises monotonically from low AoA up to ~20 degrees (where Cl is maximum at minimum sink). So reducing the AoA from best glide brings you down the Cl curve - the lift coefficient drops. Conversely, you have to fly faster (with higher speed squared) to generate the same total lift force.

workbook_chapter.title: Angle of attack and the polar of forces

diagrams.title: Polar curve of forces (Cz vs Cx)

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: accelerate, AoA decreases, Cl and Cd values decrease.

59.explanation: Increasing the angle of attack is what braking does: the wing pitches up, the lift coefficient rises, and the wing slows down. From best glide a 2-degree increase brings you closer to minimum sink - slower forward speed but longer airtime per metre of altitude.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: when angle of attack increases (braking), Cl and Cd values both rise and the wing flies slower.

60.explanation: Loading a material right up to its tensile maximum permanently deforms it: fibres are stretched past their elastic limit and microscopic damage accumulates. After such an event the part has new dimensions and reduced strength compared to its original state. That's why heavily overloaded lines must be replaced, never retensioned or re-used.

diagrams.title: Loads on a paraglider = tension only

video_clips.snippet: Sam: in certification the wing is loaded to 8G (often more) and then checked for damage afterwards.

61.explanation: A paraglider is made entirely of flexible elements - fabric and lines. Flexible things can only carry tension (pulling) - they can't resist compression, bending or torsion in any meaningful way. That's why structural design and damage analysis for a paraglider focus only on tensile loads on lines and cloth.

diagrams.title: Loads on a paraglider = tension only

62.explanation: The stagnation point is on the leading edge where the airflow divides between the upper and lower surfaces. In the SHV diagram it is marked at the front of the wing. In normal flight it sits just under the leading edge, which is why cell openings are placed there.

workbook_chapter.title: Lift and the profile of a wing

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: stagnation point is where the air decides over or under - which is exactly where the cell openings are.

63.explanation: The centre of pressure (also called the centre of thrust) is the point on the profile where all the distributed aerodynamic forces - lift and drag - can be considered to act as a single resultant force. It lies on the upper surface roughly a third of the way back from the leading edge, where lift is concentrated.

workbook_chapter.title: Lift and the profile of a wing

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the centre of pressure is where the sum of all those small force vectors acts - and equals weight in steady flight.

64.explanation: Lift on a wing is concentrated on the front half - about 80% of the load runs through the A and B risers, only about 20% through C and D combined. Of those, the D risers (furthest back) carry the smallest share. That's why steering by D risers if the brakes fail is risky: you're working with the lightly-loaded group that goes slack first.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: about 80% of the load is on A and B, only 20% on C and D - so C and D are lightly loaded.

65.explanation: Extreme manoeuvres impose heavy and sudden tension on the lines, cell walls and risers. Repeated near-limit loads stretch various components beyond their elastic range, so dimensions creep over time and the wing's certified flight characteristics drift. The lines won't all snap or quick-links crack - the damage is dimensional, slow and accumulates.

diagrams.title: Loads on a paraglider = tension only

video_clips.snippet: Sam: strong manoeuvres with a lot of G-load age the wing faster than gentle flying.

66.explanation: Pushing the speed bar pulls down the A risers most and the B risers less (sometimes a little C too) via a pulley system. Shortening the front risers tilts the wing forward, which reduces the angle of attack and increases airspeed without changing the profile much. The D risers are never shortened by the accelerator.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: pressing the speed bar pulls down the A risers, the angle of attack becomes smaller and the wing speeds up.

67.explanation: Pushing the speed bar in calm air shortens the A risers (and B to a lesser extent), lowering the angle of attack. The wing accelerates forward but the sink rate also rises - so on a still day the glide ratio actually gets worse, not better. The clear effect: more forward speed.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the speed bar reduces the angle of attack and the wing gets faster.

68.explanation: In a headwind the polar curve shifts and the best glide-over-ground tangent moves to a higher airspeed. Pressing the speed bar gets you to that new optimum: you sink a bit faster through the air but cover more ground per metre lost, so the glide ratio over the ground improves. This is exactly the situation accelerators were designed for.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the speed bar reduces the angle of attack so the wing becomes faster.

69.explanation: Trimmers are mechanical adjusters on the rear (D) risers. Letting them out lengthens the rear risers, lowering the trailing edge and reducing the angle of attack so the wing flies faster - same end effect as the speed bar but adjusted by hand and held in position. Tandems use trimmers because there's no foot room for a speed bar.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: tandems use trimmers on the rear risers - you adjust them by hand and they hold position.

70.explanation: Trimmers change the length of (usually) the rear risers, which shifts the angle of attack and flight characteristics. Because they actively modify wing geometry, the EN certification for a wing only covers configurations that were tested with the trimmers - if a wing wasn't certified with trimmers and you add them aftermarket, certification no longer applies. They aren't cosmetic and they aren't free of consequences.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: tandems use trimmers on the rear risers - hand-adjustable and held in position.

71.explanation: To fly faster you release (let out) the trimmers, which lengthens the rear risers. That lets the trailing edge rise - lowering the angle of attack and freeing the wing to accelerate. Tightening trimmers does the opposite (rear down, AoA up, slower). It's the same direction as the speed bar, just held mechanically.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: trimmers can be let out, which is what tandems use because there's no foot room for the speed bar.

72.explanation: Wing span (Spannweite) is simply the distance between the two wingtips - measured tip to tip, perpendicular to the flight direction. It can be measured either flat (wing on the ground) or projected (shadow of the inflated wing); flat is always longer.

guide_chapter.title: Geometry of a Wing & Wing Loading

diagrams.title: Flat vs projected wingspan and area

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: wing span is the distance between the two outermost wingtips, measured flat or projected.

73.explanation: Brake lines pass through small pulleys or smooth sliding rings on the rear risers so they don't scrub against the webbing. Without those guides the brake line would saw against the riser at every brake input, fraying it. Pilot habits like brake position or smoothness can't prevent that mechanical wear.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the brake line comes off the trailing edge and runs back to the handle on the rear riser.

74.explanation: Brake-line length is part of the wing's certified setup: the factory chose how much travel before reaching the stall point. Shortening or lengthening it can pre-load the trailing edge (causing parachutal stalls or losing braking authority) and voids certification. Only the manufacturer or their authorised representative can adjust it. Pilot size or harness doesn't change the brake-line baseline.

75.explanation: The brake-line length is calibrated by the manufacturer so that 'arms up' is at zero brake and the wing can use its full speed range - from full speed at the top all the way through to stall at the bottom of the travel. If brakes are too short, the wing is permanently pre-braked and you can't fly the high-speed end; if too long, you can't reach the stall to control sink.

76.explanation: With even a small brake input, the trailing edge of the wing is pulled down by the brake line itself. This lifts the rear of the canopy so the D lines aren't holding much load and visibly arc rather than pulling taut. That's the expected behaviour with brakes applied - not a sign of stretched D lines or short brakes.

77.explanation: Diagonal bracing in the harness locks the seat plate laterally. With strong bracing the pilot can't swing side to side, which both damps the feedback from the wing in turbulence and makes weight-shifting much less effective - the pilot's mass can't actually transfer to the side. Comfort and stability up, feedback and steering authority down.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

78.explanation: Diagonal cross-bracing in the harness locks the seat plate against lateral roll. Without it, the harness can swing side-to-side freely, so the pilot feels every wing movement (alive feedback in turbulence) and weight-shifting works very effectively because the pilot's mass actually transfers to one side. Cross-braced harnesses are stable but mute both.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

79.explanation: Bringing the karabiners closer together puts the pivot points near the pilot's centreline, damping lateral motion - so you feel less of the wing's micro-movements in turbulence. The downside is that closer hang points let the risers wrap around each other more easily during a collapse, so the twist risk goes up.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

80.explanation: Spreading the karabiner hang points apart gives the harness more room to swing laterally, so the pilot feels more of the wing's movement in turbulence - useful feedback for active piloting. The wider geometry also makes it geometrically harder for the risers to wrap around each other, so the twist risk drops.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

81.explanation: Karabiners mounted high up on the shoulders bring the pivot point close to the pilot, which damps lateral oscillation - so you feel less of the wing's micro-movements in turbulence. The trade-off is the high pivot makes it physically harder to lean forward and run hard during launch acceleration.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

82.explanation: Karabiners mounted low (closer to the pilot's seat) put the pivot point further from the wing, so harness oscillations are larger and the pilot feels more of the wing's motion in turbulence. The lower geometry also leaves more room to lean forward at launch, making the acceleration run easier. The trade-off: less damping than high karabiners.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

83.explanation: Adding diagonal bracing changes how the harness reacts during dynamic events - collapses, big-ear release, spirals. The braced harness damps lateral oscillations and resists twist, so the wing-pilot system behaves differently in extreme flight situations even though the wing itself, its profile and its polar curve are unchanged.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

84.explanation: Lying back in a pod-style reclined harness drops your frontal area sharply, which cuts pilot drag. Less drag means a better glide ratio - the main reason XC and competition pilots prefer this position. It's also more wind-sheltered for long flights, but it's harder to launch in and to climb into for beginners.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the prone harness gives lower drag, better glide and good wind shelter - the choice for long XC flights.

85.explanation: Lying back puts the pilot's centre of mass closer to the harness pivot line, which makes the system easier to spin about its vertical axis. In an asymmetric collapse the harness can rotate more freely and a twist becomes more likely - especially with a reactive wing. The aerodynamic glide gain comes with this handling cost.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the disadvantages of the prone harness are it's awkward to climb into and you don't have a direct view upward.

86.explanation: A back protector is passive safety - it cushions an impact you didn't prevent. It cannot replace correct launch and landing technique, weather analysis or piloting discipline. Pilots who rely on protectors as a safety net rather than as a backup take greater risks and more often end up in the kind of crash the protector was never designed to absorb.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

87.explanation: Ram-air airbags only inflate once the airflow blows into them in flight - so unpacked, they're just folded fabric and the harness is much lighter and smaller than a foam-protector version. The downside is the airbag offers no protection at launch (before it inflates), so foam wins for take-off and landing safety while airbags win for pack volume and weight.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: airbags only fill once we're in the air - they save weight and volume but give no protection at launch.

88.explanation: Foam back protection is already in its protective shape the moment you clip in - so it cushions an impact at launch, in the air or on landing. Airbags by contrast only inflate after the airflow blows into them in flight, leaving you unprotected during the run-up. The trade is foam's bigger pack volume and extra weight.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: foam protection works from the start, airbag only fills in flight - and at launch we have no protection there.

89.explanation: The accepted wing-loading range is 2.5 to 4.0 kg/m^2 for recreational pilots. At 2.0 kg/m^2 the wing is dangerously under-loaded - it will be soft, slow, easy to collapse in turbulence, and unable to develop enough internal pressure to recover cleanly. Below 2.5 is outside the certified envelope and unsafe.

90.explanation: Standard wing loading for a recreational pilot sits between 2.5 and 4 kg/m^2, so 3.5 kg/m^2 sits squarely in the normal range. Higher loading (3.5-4.2) is increasingly popular with experienced pilots because modern wings handle better when loaded up - more pressure equals more pitch authority and resistance to collapses.

91.explanation: The standard wing-loading range is 2.5-4.0 kg/m^2 with extended values up to about 4.2 for experienced pilots on modern wings. 4.5 kg/m^2 sits right at the upper limit: speed and pitch authority are great, but stall margins shrink and a collapse will be sharper. Only pilots who know the wing well should load it this high.

92.explanation: When you most need the reserve - asymmetric collapse, sudden disorientation, twisted lines - your hand has to find the release handle by feel, fast, under stress. So the single most important mounting rule is that the handle is easily seen and reached in the pilot's normal flying position. Mounting strength matters too, but no fixing point helps if you can't grab it.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

93.explanation: If the reserve attaches at the pilot's centre of gravity (same height as the pilot), the canopy doesn't automatically rotate the pilot upright after deployment - the pilot stays in whatever orientation they were in. So the pilot must actively get into the feet-down, upright landing posture before ground contact. Higher attachment points do that rotation automatically.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the priority after a reserve toss must be to land on your feet - your legs are a better protector than the harness pad.

94.explanation: AR = span squared / area. With span 12 m and area 12 m squared, AR = 144 / 12 = 12 - the largest of the four options. A wing this slender would be high-performance, with very low induced drag but demanding to fly.

guide_chapter.title: Geometry of a Wing & Wing Loading

95.explanation: Connect the reserve risers high up on the shoulder straps and behind the pilot. That geometry pulls the pilot upright once the reserve opens, putting the legs down ready to absorb the landing. Low or forward attachment points leave the pilot seated and tipped, which is the wrong body position to take the impact.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the priority is to land on your feet - the protector is never as good as your legs.

96.explanation: Reserve canopies are made of the same polyamide (nylon) ripstop as the main wing, but woven much finer and looser. The looser weave intentionally allows some stretch - that stretch absorbs the deployment shock so the lines and the pilot's body aren't slammed. Polyester or polyethylene fabrics are not used for the canopy itself.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the reserve is nylon, same cloth as the wing but woven much finer because here it's allowed to stretch.

97.explanation: Wake turbulence and vortices form behind the wing - mainly behind the trailing edge and wingtips. This is why pilots are warned not to fly close behind a heavier glider on landing: the trailing vortex can shake or even collapse a following wing.

guide_chapter.title: Lift and Drag: Profile of a Flat Object Subjected to Wind

workbook_chapter.title: Lift and the profile of a wing

diagrams.title: Where lift comes from on the profile

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: tip vortices form behind the wing and can shake a following pilot - keep distance on landing.

98.explanation: Reserve canopies must be strong enough to take the shock load when they snap open - but also intentionally elastic within defined limits so that stretch absorbs the opening shock and protects the pilot's body. Pure inelasticity would mean a violent jerk; uncontrolled elasticity would mean a sluggish, slow-opening canopy. Controlled stretch is the design target.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: with the reserve it's important that it's allowed to stretch - that's what absorbs the opening shock.

99.explanation: If a reserve sits packed for too long, the folds compress and the fabric layers can stick together (static cling, humidity, settling), which slows the canopy unwrapping at the critical moment. Regular repacking (typically every 4-6 months, with 24 hours of airing) restores loft and reliable opening speed. The strength or porosity of the cloth itself isn't changed by packing intervals.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

100.explanation: Reserve canopies are nylon ripstop, the same family of material that gets damaged by UV (degrades the fibre and any coating) and by moisture (which can rot fabric and slow opening on the next deployment). Cold and frequent packing don't materially shorten lifespan - in fact regular repacks are the recommended practice.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: UV radiation and moisture are the big enemies - pack the wing in the shade, dry it before storing.

101.explanation: Reserve lines are bundles of fibres surrounded by a sheath - water wicks into the bundle and is held against gravity by capillary action between fibres. The fabric canopy dries in hours once spread out, the containers in a day, but the lines can hold internal moisture for many days. Drying them through is critical, because wet lines can freeze, shrink and lose strength.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

102.explanation: The 5.5 m/s figure is measured on a test rig - reserve dropped from a bridge or helicopter with only a weight attached, no paraglider. In real life the deflated wing stays connected and interferes with the reserve canopy, making the whole system pendulum and raising the actual sink rate. You should expect a noticeably harder landing than the certification number suggests.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the 5.5 m/s and 1.5 m equivalent is measured without a paraglider - in real life it's more like a 2-3 m drop with the wing still attached.

103.explanation: The 5.5 m/s value is measured on a test rig: the reserve is dropped from a bridge or helicopter with just a weight on it, no paraglider. In actual emergencies the still-connected main wing interferes with the reserve canopy, swinging the system and raising the real sink rate. So the certified number is a best-case lab value, not what you should expect.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: reserves are tested without a paraglider - in real life the wing is still connected and the sink rate is higher.

104.explanation: The most important reserve criterion is that the canopy is big enough for the pilot's all-up weight - that's what keeps the sink rate below the certification target. Pack volume, weight and inner-container design matter for comfort but are secondary to sizing: a too-small reserve can never give a survivable descent. Always size to your real loaded weight.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

video_clips.snippet: Sam: a reserve gets you down at about 5.5 m/s - it's about getting safely on the ground, sizing matters.

105.explanation: A reserve opens quickly if (1) the canopy fabric has low porosity so it inflates fast, (3) the pack is fresh so folds haven't compressed/stuck, (4) you're moving fast through the air to inflate it, and (5) the handle is easy to grab so reaction time is short. Double hang points may aid stability but don't speed up the opening.

guide_chapter.title: The Harness and The Reserve Parachute

106.explanation: Drag is one of the two components of the resultant aerodynamic force (lift is the other). Drag points along the direction of the airflow / flight path, opposing the wing's motion. It's not a component of weight, and it doesn't act vertically.

guide_chapter.title: Equilibrium of Forces for a Glider in Normal Flight

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

diagrams.title: Forces on a paraglider in stable glide
Lift and drag on a flat object

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: drag runs along the airflow, perpendicular to lift - both components of the total aerodynamic force.

107.explanation: Of the fluids commonly found in a car boot, distilled water is the only one that's chemically inert toward the wing's coating and fibres. Petrol, antifreeze and screen wash all contain solvents or surfactants that dissolve or attack the polyurethane/silicone coating. If the wing must travel in the boot, store it away from any other liquids.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: temperature and acid are bad - a wet wing in a hot car boot is one of the worst combinations.

108.explanation: Ultraviolet radiation is the dominant source of fabric ageing in a paraglider. UV photons break the polymer chains in nylon fibres and the coatings, making them brittle and porous over time. Infrared is just heat (manageable), and X-ray/gamma exposure isn't a real-world flying issue. Minimising UV - by packing the wing in the shade between flights - dramatically extends life.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: UV radiation plays a big role - pack the wing and put it in the shade so the UV hours can be reduced.

109.explanation: Gloves should always be worn. Their main job is to protect the hands from line burn during launch, abrasion on rough terrain and the cold at altitude. Even in summer the brake lines under load can saw your fingers, and at 3000 m it's freezing even with sun on the ground. Modern paragliding gloves don't degrade brake feel meaningfully.

110.explanation: Statistically, head injuries in paragliding overwhelmingly happen during the launch run and landing - tripping, hitting the slope, low-altitude impacts. The helmet's main job is to absorb those low-energy ground impacts. Mid-air collisions with other aircraft or falls from altitude are catastrophic regardless of helmet, so the helmet is specifically designed for the take-off/landing phase.

111.explanation: Full-face (integral) helmets cover the face and chin in addition to the skull, so they protect the face and jaw during the launch and landing impacts that are most common. Modern composite full-face helmets are both light and strong. Bicycle, climbing and jet helmets give less coverage and are not optimal for paragliding's typical impact patterns.

112.explanation: An anemometer measures the speed of the air flowing past it - i.e. the wing's airspeed relative to the surrounding air mass, not its ground speed and not wind direction. It's not the same as a GPS speed reading, which gives speed over ground (and includes any wind drift).

113.explanation: The anemometer needs clean air to measure airspeed accurately. Mounting it on a riser, on the pilot's body, or up in the lines puts it in the disturbed flow around the pilot or the wing. About 1 m below the pilot's foot is far enough from the pilot's slipstream and harness drag to read the actual airflow the wing is seeing.

114.explanation: A variometer measures and reports the glider's vertical speed - climb rate in thermals, sink rate elsewhere. It does so by tracking the rate of change in atmospheric pressure with time. An audible vario is the single most useful instrument for a paraglider pilot. It does not measure horizontal speed or air density.

115.explanation: A variometer doesn't measure speed directly - it measures atmospheric pressure and reports its rate of change. Since pressure decreases steadily with altitude, a falling pressure means you're climbing and a rising pressure means you're sinking. The numerical readout in m/s is computed from that pressure trend.

116.explanation: A wing that automatically returns to trim after the speedbar is released - without pilot input - has stable characteristics. Like a ball at the bottom of a bowl: a disturbance moves it temporarily but it always rolls back to centre. This is the desired property manufacturers aim for, especially on school wings.

diagrams.title: Stable, unstable and indifferent flight characteristics

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: stable wing returns to trim by itself after a disturbance - like a ball at the bottom of a bowl.

117.explanation: A strengthening or approaching high pressure pushes local pressure up. An altimeter calibrated in the morning interprets the rising pressure as a descent - so by the afternoon its altitude reading is too low compared to the actual height. Always recalibrate before each flight, especially when the weather pattern is changing.

118.explanation: An altimeter derives altitude from atmospheric pressure: when local pressure drops it concludes you went higher. If the high pressure system is weakening, pressure falls during the day - so a morning-calibrated altimeter reads too high by the afternoon. Always recalibrate before each flight to avoid this drift.

119.explanation: EN certification is a European Norm scheme. It is voluntary - manufacturers aren't legally forced to certify - but in practice SHV and most national bodies expect EN compliance, and BAZL requires it for exam wings. It is per wing model (one certificate per type), not per individual airframe.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the EN certification mark is valid worldwide except in Germany where LTF still applies.

120.explanation: Glide ratio is the lift-to-drag ratio (Cl / Cd). It's one of four equivalent ways to express the same number: lift/drag, Cl/Cd, horizontal speed / sink rate, and horizontal distance / altitude lost. Higher lift relative to drag = better glide = flying further for each metre of altitude.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: glide ratio can be calculated four ways - distance/height, speeds, lift/drag, or Cl/Cd.

121.explanation: Glide ratio is the ratio of the lift coefficient Cl to the drag coefficient Cd. Since Cl and Cd are proportional to lift and drag (with the same speed, density and area factors), Cl/Cd = lift/drag = glide ratio. This is one of the four equivalent definitions.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: glide ratio equals lift over drag, or equivalently Cl over Cd.

122.explanation: In Switzerland, flying with an EN-certified wing is legally required only for the official BAZL/OFAC exams. Outside of exams it's strongly recommended but not statutorily compulsory - though SHV insurance and most schools effectively require it. Across the rest of Europe EN is the de facto standard, with Germany still on the older LTF as the exception.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: the EN certification mark is valid worldwide - the only exception is Germany, which is still on LTF.

123.explanation: EN certification tests a brand new wing of a specific model: the static and shock-load tests check structural strength (8G minimum, often more), and the in-flight manoeuvre sequence (24 manoeuvres scored across 90 points) classifies its flight characteristics A/B/C/D. It's a one-time, type-level certification - it says nothing about how the wing holds up after wear, age or damage.

video_clips.snippet: Sam: in cert, 24 manoeuvres are flown and 90 individual points awarded - that's how you get an A or B wing.

124.explanation: Distance = glide ratio x altitude lost = 8 x 0.8 km = 6.4 km. The wing flies 8 m horizontally for each metre of altitude lost, so from 800 m the longest possible distance in calm air is 6.4 km. In practice you would plan more conservatively (glide ratio ~5-6) to leave margin for sink and wind.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: glide ratio 9 from 1000 m gives 9 km - but in practice plan conservatively with 5 or 6.

125.explanation: Distance = glide ratio x altitude = 12 x 2.4 km = 28.8 km. A glide ratio of 12 is more typical of high-performance / competition wings than school wings. Even so, the calm-air calculation is an upper bound; real flight always involves some sink and headwind.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

126.explanation: Glide ratio = horizontal distance / altitude lost = 7.0 km / 1.4 km = 5. The wing has travelled 5 m forward for every metre of altitude given up. Always convert to the same units (here both in km) before dividing.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

127.explanation: Glide angle is the angle between the trajectory and the horizon. A flatter glide (more horizontal distance per metre lost) means a SMALLER glide angle. So as the glide ratio increases, the glide angle decreases. This is the classic SHV gotcha - 'good glide' = 'small angle'.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: the glide angle is NOT the glide ratio - large glide ratio means small glide angle.

128.explanation: Inverse of the previous: if the glide angle (the steepness of descent) increases, you're sinking more per metre of forward travel, so the glide ratio (distance / altitude) decreases. Steeper angle = worse glide; flatter angle = better glide.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: bigger glide angle = smaller glide ratio - they move in opposite directions.

129.explanation: The graph that plots horizontal airspeed against sink rate across the whole speed range of the glider is the speed polar curve (Geschwindigkeitspolare). The four key points - stall, minimum sink, best glide, Vmax - lie on this curve. A barogram is altitude vs time; emagram is meteorological.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan introduces the speed polar - horizontal speed vs sink rate over the full speed range.

130.explanation: On the speed polar, best glide angle (Fmax) is identified by the tangent from the origin to the curve. For the SHV reference glider this lands at around 9 m/s horizontal airspeed (approximately 33 km/h) - the speed for the maximum glide ratio in calm air. That's the trim point at which the wing flies furthest per metre lost.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: best glide is where the tangent from the origin touches the polar - in this example around 9 m/s.

131.explanation: Read off the polar: best glide point is at ~9.2 m/s horizontal speed and ~1.7 m/s sink rate. Glide ratio = 9.2 / 1.7 ≈ 5.3. This is the maximum glide ratio for the reference glider - flatter than any other point on the polar by definition.

132.explanation: Minimum sink (Tmin) is the apex of the polar curve - the highest point - found by drawing a horizontal tangent. For the SHV reference glider that is at about 7.2 m/s (~26 km/h) horizontal speed and ~1.5 m/s sink. Used when you want to stay up the longest, e.g. in weak lift.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: minimum sink point - the wing sinks the least, around 15-20% braked.

133.explanation: At minimum sink: ~7.2 m/s horizontal / ~1.5 m/s sink ≈ 4.6 - 4.8. Min sink has a worse glide ratio than best glide, even though you sink slower in absolute terms. You're trading distance for airtime.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: at minimum sink the glide ratio is worse than at best glide, but you sink the least.

134.explanation: Minimum flight speed - just above stall - is the leftmost point on the polar curve where lift is still being generated. For the reference glider this is around 5.6 m/s (~20 km/h). Below this the wing stalls and ceases to fly forward.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: stall is the leftmost point - too slow, flow detaches, wing no longer flies.
Air Active stall-point drill: pull brakes deep and the wing transitions from forward flight to deep stall.

135.explanation: At minimum speed the sink rate has shot up dramatically (close to stall). Roughly 5.6 m/s horizontal over 2.8 m/s sink gives glide ratio = 2.0. Heavy braking ruins the glide - the wing falls almost as fast as it moves forward.

136.explanation: Vmax (full speedbar): ~11.3 m/s horizontal / ~3 m/s sink ≈ 3.7. Max speed comes with a heavy sink-rate penalty - the glide ratio is much worse than at trim. Use accelerator only when fighting headwind or sink, not for maximum glide in calm air.

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: Vmax has a smaller glide ratio than Fmax - you're trading glide for speed.
Air Active accelerated-flight drill: how to set up and use the speedbar.

137.explanation: In a strong headwind the best-glide tangent shifts forward on the polar - to maintain the highest glide ratio over the ground, you must accelerate. With 8 m/s of headwind (close to the wing's own trim speed) the new tangent often touches the polar near Vmax. Push the speedbar fully (with safety margin to the slope).

guide_chapter.title: Polar Speeds in Moving Air Mass

diagrams.title: Polar curve with wind and sink

video_clips.snippet: Stefan walks through the headwind polar shift - the tangent moves forward to maximum speed.
Air Active: how to engage the accelerator for accelerated flight.

138.explanation: Glide ratio is the ratio of distance to altitude lost. From the same altitude, a glider with ratio 10 flies twice as far as one with ratio 5 - 10/5 = 2. Sink rates depend on airspeed too, not just glide ratio, so the sink-rate options in the question are not directly derivable.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

139.explanation: In sinking air, shift the origin of the polar down by the sink rate. The new tangent for best glide-over-ground touches the polar at a higher airspeed than best glide in calm air - so accelerate. Same rule as for headwind: fly faster to get out of the bad air mass and improve ground-relative glide.

guide_chapter.title: Polar Speeds in Moving Air Mass

diagrams.title: Polar curve with wind and sink

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: in sink, accelerate - get out of the bad air and your glide ratio improves dramatically.

140.explanation: In a headwind, the polar origin shifts to the right (subtract wind from ground speed). The new best-glide tangent touches the polar at a higher airspeed - so accelerate. The stronger the headwind, the more accelerator you need to maximise glide over the ground.

guide_chapter.title: Polar Speeds in Moving Air Mass

diagrams.title: Polar curve with wind and sink

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: headwind shifts the tangent forward - you have to accelerate to maintain max ground glide.

141.explanation: Relative to the air mass, the wing flies at the same airspeed (36 km/h) and sinks at the same 1 m/s. But the whole air mass is itself sinking at 1 m/s, so the wing's sink rate over the ground is 1 + 1 = 2 m/s. Air speed stays the same; only the ground-relative sink changes.

guide_chapter.title: Polar Speeds in Moving Air Mass

diagrams.title: Polar curve with wind and sink

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: 10 m/s forward, 1 m/s wing sink, plus 1 m/s sinking air = total 2 m/s sink, glide drops from 10 to 5.

142.explanation: Forward speed still 10 m/s, but total sink rate over the ground is now 2 m/s (wing's 1 m/s + air mass's 1 m/s). New glide ratio = 10 / 2 = 5. Even moderate sink halves the glide ratio of an excellent glider - a powerful reminder that 'manufacturer glide ratio' rarely matches reality.

guide_chapter.title: Polar Speeds in Moving Air Mass

diagrams.title: Polar curve with wind and sink

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: 10 / 2 = 5 - the glider's glide ratio collapses from 10 to 5 just because of a 1 m/s sinking air mass.

143.explanation: Sink rate doesn't change with horizontal wind - the wing's behaviour relative to the air mass is unaffected. Only the ground speed changes: 15 m/s air speed - 5 m/s headwind = 10 m/s ground speed (= 36 km/h). Sink stays at 2 m/s.

guide_chapter.title: Polar Speeds in Moving Air Mass

diagrams.title: Polar curve with wind and sink

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: horizontal wind only changes ground speed - sink rate stays the same.

144.explanation: Ground speed = 15 - 5 = 10 m/s, sink = 2 m/s, so glide-over-ground = 10 / 2 = 5. In calm air the same wing would have glide 15/2 = 7.5; the headwind has reduced effective glide by a third. This is why you accelerate in headwind to push the ratio back up.

guide_chapter.title: Polar Speeds in Moving Air Mass

diagrams.title: Polar curve with wind and sink

145.explanation: Sink rate = horizontal speed / glide ratio = 12 / 10 = 1.2 m/s. The four equivalent definitions of glide ratio let you solve for any single missing quantity if the other two are given.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

146.explanation: Glide ratio 8 means 8 m forward per 1 m lost, so altitude lost = distance / glide ratio = 1600 / 8 = 200 m. Simple division - any conservative crossing plan would build in margin above this minimum.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

147.explanation: Forward speed = glide ratio x sink rate = 9 x 1 m/s = 9 m/s. Converting: 9 x 3.6 = 32.4 km/h. Always remember the m/s to km/h conversion factor of 3.6 - critical for these questions.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: conversion factor 3.6 - 36 km/h divided by 3.6 = 10 m/s; the m/s and ratio together give glide.

148.explanation: Glide ratio = forward speed / sink rate = 9 / 1.5 = 6.0. Straight application of the definition. Don't be fooled by km/h - first convert both speeds to the same units, or note that 32.4 km/h divided by something in km/h gives the same ratio as the m/s calculation.

workbook_chapter.title: Equilibrium of forces and the glide ratio

149.explanation: Airspeed at a given angle of attack depends only on wing loading and the air around it - not on whether the whole air mass is rising or sinking. So the airspeed for minimum sink is the same in lift, sink and calm air. What changes is the ground-relative sink rate, not the airspeed.

guide_chapter.title: Polar Speeds in Moving Air Mass

diagrams.title: Polar curve with wind and sink

150.explanation: Load factor (in G) is the ratio of the actual loading on the glider during a manoeuvre to the static weight at rest on the ground. In a tight turn the centrifugal force adds to weight, so the wing 'feels' heavier; the load factor measures that increase.

guide_chapter.title: Equilibrium of Forces for a Glider while Turning & Load Factor

diagrams.title: Forces and load factor in a turn

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: load factor in a turn - at 45 degrees bank it's 1.4 G, at 60 degrees 2 G, at 70 degrees 3 G.

151.explanation: Load factor = actual load / normal weight = 250 kg / 100 kg = 2.5 G. The wing carries 2.5 times its straight-flight weight - corresponding to a bank angle around 65 degrees. Load factor is dimensionless (always expressed as a multiple of G).

guide_chapter.title: Equilibrium of Forces for a Glider while Turning & Load Factor

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: at 60 degrees bank you experience 2 G, at 70 degrees 3 G - hanging a second wing has the same effect.

152.explanation: Entering a turn introduces centrifugal force, which adds to weight - the effective load on the wing increases. To balance this larger 'effective weight', the wing must fly faster, which shifts the entire speed polar up: trim speed, max speed AND minimum (stall) speed all increase. Wing loading rises proportionally to the load factor.

guide_chapter.title: Equilibrium of Forces for a Glider while Turning & Load Factor

diagrams.title: Forces and load factor in a turn

video_clips.snippet: Stefan: load multiples raise wing loading, the polar shifts forward and minimum speed rises with everything else.
Air Active turn flying: weight shift first, then brake - load factor and minimum speed both rise.
